turns.content: I'm back at my old job and trying to rebuild this side project with Axum, but I keep getting confused about how the framework handles conditional requests. Can you explain how to use the If-None-Match header in a web server?

When handling an incoming request with the If-None-Match header, your application should parse that header value and compare it against previously stored ETags for the resource. The core logic involves reading the request headers from axum's Request type, extracting the If-None-Match string, then computing or retrieving a new ETag based on the current state of your data. If these two values match exactly, you should skip generating the full response body and instead send a 304 Not Modified status code to save bandwidth.
I think you're confusing me with all that jargon about headers and bodies. Why does sending just a 304 status code actually help performance? Isn't it just wasting time checking if the data changed?

You're right to question why we do this at all, but the real benefit is avoiding unnecessary network traffic when nothing has changed since the client last fetched the resource. Without this mechanism, your server would have to generate a full HTML response every single time even if the underlying database hasn't moved in hours or days. By sending only the 304 status code and letting the client discard the previous cached version, you reduce CPU usage on your server because it skips the expensive serialization of large objects like Vec<String> or complex structs into JSON, which is handled by serde_json. This also saves the client's bandwidth since they don't receive kilobytes of text when nothing new has arrived.
